evals.prompt: I'm about to make my private repo public on GitHub. It's a Node.js/Express app that has been in development for 6 months. Scan for any secrets that might be committed and give me a remediation plan.
evals.expected_output: Gitleaks scan command, vendor pattern list to check, file types to flag, remediation steps (rotate, remove from history, gitignore, pre-commit hook), prevention setup

assertions.name: scan_command
assertions.description: Provides gitleaks detect command or equivalent scanning approach

assertions.name: vendor_patterns
assertions.description: Lists specific vendor patterns to check (AWS keys, GitHub tokens, Stripe keys, etc.)

assertions.name: file_flags
assertions.description: Flags .env files, .pem/.key files, and other sensitive file types

assertions.name: rotate_first
assertions.description: Emphasizes rotating/revoking secrets BEFORE removing from git history

assertions.name: history_cleanup
assertions.description: Includes git history cleanup (BFG or git-filter-repo)

assertions.name: prevention_setup
assertions.description: Sets up pre-commit hook and .gitignore to prevent future leaks

evals.prompt: I'm reviewing a PR for our Django + AWS project that modifies settings.py and adds a new deploy.sh script. Can you check if any secrets might be in these changes before I approve?
evals.expected_output: Focused scan on settings.py patterns (SECRET_KEY, DB credentials, AWS keys) and deploy script (hardcoded credentials, key paths), with PR-specific guidance

assertions.name: scans_settings_py
assertions.description: Checks settings.py for Django SECRET_KEY, DATABASE config, AWS credentials

assertions.name: scans_deploy_script
assertions.description: Checks deploy.sh for hardcoded credentials, SSH keys, or API tokens

assertions.name: distinguishes_env_refs
assertions.description: Distinguishes os.environ references from hardcoded values (env refs are OK)

assertions.name: pr_specific_guidance
assertions.description: Focuses on the changed files, not a full repo scan

assertions.name: recommends_ci_check
assertions.description: Suggests adding secret scanning to CI for future PRs

assertions.name: checks_aws_patterns
assertions.description: Specifically checks for AKIA keys, secret access keys, region strings

evals.prompt: Set up a complete secret prevention system for our new TypeScript monorepo. We need pre-commit hooks, CI scanning in GitHub Actions, and a .env.example template. We use AWS, Stripe, and SendGrid APIs.
evals.expected_output: Pre-commit config with Gitleaks, GitHub Actions workflow, .env.example with all three services, .gitignore updates, developer guidelines

assertions.name: pre_commit_hook
assertions.description: Provides .pre-commit-config.yaml with Gitleaks hook configuration

assertions.name: ci_workflow
assertions.description: GitHub Actions workflow that runs gitleaks on PRs

assertions.name: env_example
assertions.description: .env.example with placeholder values for AWS, Stripe, and SendGrid

assertions.name: gitignore_updates
assertions.description: Adds .env, *.pem, *.key to .gitignore

assertions.name: covers_all_three_services
assertions.description: Mentions patterns for AWS (AKIA), Stripe (sk_live_), and SendGrid (SG.) specifically

assertions.name: developer_guidelines
assertions.description: Guidance on where to store secrets (env vars, secret manager) and what not to commit

evals.prompt: Emergency! A junior dev committed our production .env file with real database credentials and Stripe live keys. It was 3 commits ago. The repo is private with 5 team members. What do I do right now?
evals.expected_output: Emergency remediation: rotate immediately, clean history, verify, notify team. Urgent tone with clear step ordering.

assertions.name: rotate_first_emphasis
assertions.description: Step 1 is rotating all exposed secrets — DB password, Stripe keys — BEFORE cleaning history

assertions.name: specific_rotation_steps
assertions.description: Names the specific credentials to rotate (DB password, Stripe live key) with provider instructions

assertions.name: history_cleanup
assertions.description: Provides BFG or git-filter-repo commands to remove .env from all 3 commits

assertions.name: team_notification
assertions.description: Recommends notifying all 5 team members to re-pull after force push

assertions.name: verification_scan
assertions.description: Re-run gitleaks after cleanup to verify clean history

assertions.name: urgency_conveyed
assertions.description: Treats this as urgent — rotation before cleanup, not the reverse

evals.prompt: Audit our Terraform repository for leaked secrets. We have AWS provider configs in providers.tf, RDS and ElastiCache modules in modules/, some .tfvars files, and I'm not sure if our state files are committed. Check everything.
evals.expected_output: Checks tfstate files, tfvars for credentials, provider blocks for hardcoded keys, recommends remote state backend and env var injection

assertions.name: checks_tfstate
assertions.description: Checks if .tfstate files are committed (they contain all secrets in plaintext)

assertions.name: checks_tfvars
assertions.description: Checks .tfvars files for hardcoded passwords, access keys, or tokens

assertions.name: checks_provider_blocks
assertions.description: Checks provider blocks for hardcoded access_key/secret_key instead of env vars

assertions.name: recommends_remote_state
assertions.description: Recommends S3/GCS remote state backend instead of local state files

assertions.name: recommends_env_vars
assertions.description: Suggests using TF_VAR_ environment variables or Vault instead of hardcoded values

assertions.name: gitignore_for_tf
assertions.description: Adds *.tfstate, *.tfvars (except examples), and .terraform/ to .gitignore
